title: Adrenal Crisis (Addisonian Crisis)
subtitle: Endocrine Emergency & Glucocorticoid-First Resuscitation

patient.name: P. Wilson
patient.admitDate: Today, 1100 (EMS transport, found obtunded by daughter)

labs.v: Pending (expected critically low for stress state)
labs.r: >18 mcg/dL in stress; <5 mcg/dL diagnostic of AI

labs.v: Sinus tachycardia 138 with peaked T-waves V2-V4 (hyperkalemia), prolonged PR 224 ms

nursesNote: 1130 — 54 y/o female with known autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) diagnosed 6 years ago. Maintenance regimen: hydrocortisone 20 mg PO at 0700 + 10 mg PO at 1500, plus fludrocortisone 0.1 mg PO daily. Wears a medical alert bracelet stating 'Addison's disease — needs steroids in emergency.' Family reports 4 days of viral gastroenteritis with frequent vomiting and watery diarrhea. Per daughter, client has been unable to keep oral medications down for the past 36 hours and 'thought she could just rest and ride it out — didn't want to bother anyone.' Today the daughter found her obtunded on the couch, barely arousable, called 911. PMH: Addison's disease (autoimmune), Hashimoto's thyroiditis on levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily, GERD. EMS report: scene SpO2 92%, glucose 48 (administered IV D50W 25 g en route), HR 134 sinus tach, BP 72/40 unresponsive to first 1L 0.9% NS bolus, IV NS continued during transport. On ED arrival: client lethargic, oriented to person only, withdraws to noxious stimulus, follows simple commands intermittently. NOTABLE HYPERPIGMENTATION of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, knuckles, and an old C-section surgical scar — classic for primary AI (chronic ACTH elevation drives MSH production). Skin warm centrally but mottled and cool at knees and feet. Capillary refill 5 seconds. Heart sounds regular tachycardic without S3/S4 or murmur. Lungs clear bilaterally. Abdomen soft, mildly diffusely tender, hyperactive bowel sounds. No focal neurological deficits when arousable. Cardiac monitor: sinus tachycardia 138 with peaked T-waves V2-V4 on 12-lead ECG. UOP 30 mL since EMS arrival. Daughter very distressed: 'I should have brought her in days ago.'
providerOrders: Activate adrenal crisis protocol; ICU pending bed
Continuous pulse oximetry, frequent (q5min) BP cycling until stable
STAT cortisol level AND ACTH level — DRAW BEFORE hydrocortisone if access allows; do NOT delay hydrocortisone for this
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV PUSH STAT — single most time-critical intervention
Follow with hydrocortisone 200 mg IV continuous infusion over 24 hours (OR 50 mg IV q6h)
0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus over 30 min (in addition to EMS's 1 L); then transition to D5NS at 200 mL/hr
POC glucose q15min initially; D50W 25 g IV push for recurrent glucose <70

Hyperkalemia management: calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly (membrane stabilization for K+ 6.4 with peaked T-waves); regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25 g IV; albuterol 10 mg nebulized; consider sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) 30 g PO
Repeat BMP and K+ q2h until stable
STAT CBC with diff, ABG, lactate, blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites (rule out concurrent sepsis as trigger)
TSH, free T4 (concurrent Hashimoto's; severe hypothyroidism can compound)
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics (ceftriaxone) if sepsis is suspected — do NOT delay if febrile or signs of infection
Indwelling Foley catheter for hourly UOP monitoring
NPO initially while obtunded; advance to clears as mentation and N/V resolve
Pre-discharge: stress dose education for client and family; prescribe injectable hydrocortisone (Solu-Cortef 100 mg) IM emergency kit for future severe illness; reinforce medical alert bracelet

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate nursing attention.

opts.text: Hypotension 76/44 unresponsive to 1 L IV NS bolus from EMS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — refractory shock pattern is the hallmark of adrenal crisis. Glucocorticoid deficiency causes vascular hyporesponsiveness to catecholamines; the vessels cannot constrict appropriately even when filled. Fluid bolus alone WILL NOT WORK — hydrocortisone is required. This is the single most distinguishing feature.

opts.text: Known autoimmune Addison's disease for 6 years; unable to take oral hydrocortisone for 36 hours due to vomiting; did not stress-dose during illness
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — establishes the diagnosis. Any chronic steroid user who becomes acutely ill and cannot absorb their replacement dose is at IMMEDIATE risk for adrenal crisis. The classic teaching: 'sick day rules' — double or triple the dose during minor illness, switch to IV for vomiting that prevents PO absorption. This client did neither.

opts.text: Hyperpigmentation of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, knuckles, and old surgical scar
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — distinguishes PRIMARY adrenal insufficiency (Addison's) from secondary AI (pituitary or chronic exogenous steroid suppression). In primary AI, the failed adrenals don't make cortisol → pituitary releases massive ACTH → ACTH's precursor (POMC) is also processed into MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) → hyperpigmentation. Secondary AI does NOT have this finding.

opts.text: Sodium 122 mEq/L with concurrent potassium 6.4 mEq/L
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the pathognomonic electrolyte pattern of primary AI. Aldosterone normally reabsorbs sodium and excretes potassium in the distal tubule. Without aldosterone (mineralocorticoid deficiency), the kidneys waste sodium (→ hyponatremia + volume depletion) and retain potassium (→ hyperkalemia). This pattern is essentially diagnostic in the right clinical context.

opts.text: Hyperkalemia 6.4 with peaked T-waves in V2-V4 and prolonged PR 224 ms on ECG
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — immediately life-threatening. The combination of K+ >6 with ECG changes mandates urgent cardiac membrane stabilization (calcium gluconate) and K+ shifting (insulin/D50, albuterol). Untreated, can progress to widened QRS → sine wave → asystole within minutes.

opts.text: Hypoglycemia 52 mg/dL
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Cortisol is one of the four counter-regulatory hormones that maintain glucose (along with glucagon, epinephrine, and growth hormone). Cortisol deficiency removes this safety net — clients become hypoglycemic, especially when not eating. Treat with D50W IV and continuous D5-containing fluid; recheck q15min until stable.

opts.text: Eosinophilia 8% on differential
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — under-recognized finding. Cortisol normally suppresses eosinophil counts (which is why steroid administration drops eos within hours). In cortisol deficiency, eosinophils are uninhibited → mild eosinophilia. A small but clinically useful clue in undiagnosed AI.

opts.text: Mental status: lethargic, oriented to person only, withdraws to noxious stimulus
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — multifactorial encephalopathy. Contributors: hypoglycemia (cerebral fuel deficit), hyponatremia (cerebral edema), hypoperfusion (oxygen delivery), and cortisol deficiency itself (which has direct CNS effects on alertness and mood). Improves rapidly with combined treatment.

opts.text: Sinus tachycardia 138 with mottled cool extremities and cap refill 5 sec
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Circulation. Compensatory tachycardia for hypotension; peripheral vasoconstriction is the body's attempt to maintain central perfusion despite glucocorticoid-deficient vascular bed. Lactate 3.4 confirms cellular hypoperfusion. The persistence despite fluids confirms the diagnosis.

opts.text: Lactate 3.4 mmol/L with metabolic acidosis (pH 7.30, HCO3 18)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE. Lactate reflects tissue hypoperfusion + anaerobic metabolism. Will improve with hydrocortisone (restoring vascular tone) AND volume (filling the vascular space) AND glucose (providing aerobic fuel). All three are needed.

opts.text: Temperature 99.6°F
opts.rat: NOT a priority cue in itself, but raises the possibility of concurrent infection as a precipitating trigger. Draw blood cultures and consider empiric antibiotics if any other infection signs emerge. Don't anchor on 'just adrenal crisis' if sepsis features develop.

opts.text: Wearing a medical alert bracelet stating 'Addison's disease — needs steroids in emergency'
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — life-saving information. Medical alert jewelry is critical in clients with adrenal insufficiency because they may be found unconscious; first responders see the bracelet, administer empiric IM hydrocortisone, and save the client's life. Reinforce wearing at discharge.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying pathophysiology.

opts.text: In primary adrenal insufficiency, BOTH cortisol AND aldosterone production fail — cortisol deficiency causes hypoglycemia, GI symptoms, eosinophilia, and vascular hyporesponsiveness; aldosterone deficiency causes hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and volume depletion
opts.rat: CORRECT — the dual-deficiency physiology that defines Addison's disease and distinguishes it from secondary AI (where aldosterone is preserved because the renin-angiotensin axis is independent of pituitary ACTH). This explains why primary AI patients have a more dramatic electrolyte picture than secondary AI patients.

opts.text: Hyperpigmentation in primary AI results from the failed adrenals not providing negative feedback to the pituitary → ACTH levels rise → ACTH's precursor proopiomelanocortin (POMC) is also processed into MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone) → increased melanin production
opts.rat: CORRECT — the elegant biochemistry explaining a classic exam finding. Hyperpigmentation appears in sun-exposed areas, scars made AFTER the disease developed, mucous membranes (buccal, gingival), palmar creases, areolas, and pressure points. This finding does NOT occur in secondary AI.

opts.text: Hypotension in adrenal crisis is REFRACTORY to fluids because glucocorticoids are required for normal vascular smooth muscle responsiveness to catecholamines — without cortisol, vessels cannot constrict appropriately even when intravascular volume is restored
opts.rat: CORRECT — THE most important pathophysiologic concept. Cortisol upregulates and sensitizes alpha-1 adrenergic receptors. Without it, endogenous and exogenous catecholamines have markedly reduced effect. This is why hydrocortisone (NOT fluids alone, NOT vasopressors alone) is the cornerstone of adrenal crisis treatment.

opts.text: The 4 days of vomiting and diarrhea created a 'perfect storm': inability to absorb oral hydrocortisone replacement, increased physiologic stress (cortisol demand normally rises 2–5x during illness), volume losses, and hypoglycemia from poor PO intake
opts.rat: CORRECT — the typical adrenal crisis trigger. Other classic precipitants: surgery without stress-dose coverage, severe infection (sepsis), trauma, MI, sudden withdrawal of chronic steroids. The lesson: any chronic steroid user MUST stress-dose during illness or switch to parenteral if vomiting.

opts.text: Hydrocortisone is the drug of choice for acute adrenal crisis because at high doses (>100 mg/24 hr) it has SIGNIFICANT mineralocorticoid activity in addition to glucocorticoid activity — so fludrocortisone usually does not need to be given separately during the acute phase
opts.rat: CORRECT — practical pharmacology. Hydrocortisone 100 mg has roughly equivalent mineralocorticoid effect to 0.1 mg fludrocortisone. Once the dose drops below 50 mg/day during taper, fludrocortisone is added or reinstituted. Dexamethasone is sometimes preferred when the diagnosis isn't yet confirmed because it does NOT interfere with the cosyntropin stimulation test.

opts.text: Findings represent severe sepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, leukocytosis, lactic acidosis) and should be treated primarily with the Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle
opts.rat: INCORRECT — anchoring error. While sepsis can mimic adrenal crisis (and can also PRECIPITATE adrenal crisis in known AI clients), the pattern here is more consistent with primary AI: known Addison's, missed stress doses, hyperpigmentation, classic electrolytes (low Na, high K), hypoglycemia, eosinophilia. Treat both possibilities (steroids + fluids + empiric antibiotics + cultures) but recognize that this is adrenal crisis first.

opts.text: Hydrocortisone should be held until cortisol and ACTH levels confirm the diagnosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ INCORRECT — DANGEROUS. Adrenal crisis kills via hours of delay. Draw cortisol/ACTH BEFORE giving hydrocortisone IF doing so does not delay treatment more than a few minutes (single venipuncture). If confirmatory testing isn't immediately feasible, GIVE THE HYDROCORTISONE FIRST. Dexamethasone is an alternative when concerned about test interference; the cosyntropin stimulation test can be done after the first dose.

opts.text: Vasopressors (norepinephrine, vasopressin) are the FIRST-line therapy for the refractory hypotension and hydrocortisone is just a supportive measure
opts.rat: INCORRECT. Hydrocortisone IS the first-line therapy. Vasopressors are largely INEFFECTIVE without cortisol because the vascular bed is hyporesponsive. After hydrocortisone, if BP is still inadequate, norepinephrine is the second-line agent (and will work better once steroids restore vascular tone).

opts.text: Rapid 3% hypertonic saline correction is indicated for the sodium of 122 mEq/L
opts.rat: INCORRECT in this client. Hypertonic saline is reserved for severely symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures, coma, or imminent herniation. This client's mental status will improve with restoration of intravascular volume (with isotonic fluids), glucose, and cortisol. Rapid sodium correction (>8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours) risks central pontine myelinolysis — a devastating, often irreversible neurologic injury.

opts.text: The hyperkalemia will resolve on its own once volume status improves; no specific treatment is required acutely
opts.rat: INCORRECT — K+ 6.4 with peaked T-waves is a medical emergency requiring immediate cardiac membrane stabilization (calcium gluconate) and K+-shifting therapy (insulin/D50, albuterol, kayexalate). While hydrocortisone will eventually correct the mineralocorticoid deficiency, that takes hours; the hyperkalemia could cause a lethal arrhythmia in minutes.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank nursing concerns from highest to lowest priority
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority — most life-threatening) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Risk for Cardiovascular Collapse r/t glucocorticoid-deficient vascular hyporesponsiveness and volume depletion
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Circulation (ABCs). Refractory hypotension despite fluids confirms the depth of glucocorticoid deficiency. Without immediate hydrocortisone, the client cannot maintain perfusion and will progress to cardiovascular collapse. Hydrocortisone is the ONLY intervention that addresses the root cause.

opts.text: Risk for Lethal Arrhythmia r/t hyperkalemia 6.4 with peaked T-waves and prolonged PR
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. ECG changes confirm the K+ is affecting cardiac conduction. Without urgent membrane stabilization (calcium) and K+ shifting (insulin/D50, albuterol), progression to widened QRS, sine wave, and asystole can occur within minutes.

opts.text: Acute Hypoglycemia r/t cortisol-deficient gluconeogenesis
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Glucose 52 even AFTER 25 g D50W from EMS suggests ongoing hypoglycemia driver. Brain depends on glucose; profound hypoglycemia causes seizures and irreversible cerebral injury. Continuous D5-containing fluids plus serial POC glucose checks address this.

opts.text: Severe Hyponatremia r/t aldosterone-deficient renal sodium wasting
opts.rat: #4 — Important but secondary to immediate hemodynamic and arrhythmia risks. Will correct gradually with volume restoration using isotonic fluids and hydrocortisone (which has mineralocorticoid effect at high doses). Avoid rapid correction to prevent CPM.

opts.text: Knowledge Deficit r/t sick day rules and stress dosing for chronic adrenal insufficiency
opts.rat: #5 — LOWEST in this acute moment but CRITICAL for safe discharge. This is the reason for the crisis. Without comprehensive teaching (sick day rules, stress dosing protocol, injectable hydrocortisone kit for emergencies, medical alert bracelet reinforcement), recurrence is highly likely. Family education matters because the client may be obtunded next time.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH intervention, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this client at this moment.

opts.text: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV PUSH STAT, followed by 200 mg IV continuous infusion over 24 hours
opts.rat: Single most time-critical intervention. Acts within 30 minutes; full effect within 4–6 hours. The 100 mg loading dose ensures rapid receptor saturation. Continuous infusion maintains stable levels (vs. peaks and troughs of q6h bolus dosing, although that's also acceptable). At >100 mg/24 hr, mineralocorticoid effect covers aldosterone deficiency.

opts.text: Draw cortisol AND ACTH levels BEFORE administering hydrocortisone if access allows (single venipuncture); do NOT delay hydrocortisone if drawing labs would take more than a few minutes
opts.rat: Pre-treatment cortisol and ACTH provide diagnostic confirmation: in primary AI, cortisol is low AND ACTH is high; in secondary AI, both are low. Once hydrocortisone is given, the cortisol assay is unreliable. BUT: never delay hydrocortisone more than a few minutes for testing — the test confirms what you already suspect clinically; the steroid saves the life.

opts.text: 0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus, followed by D5NS at 200 mL/hr maintenance
opts.rat: Restores intravascular volume; D5 component addresses concurrent hypoglycemia. NS gradually replaces sodium without rapid correction (avoid CPM). Hydrocortisone alone cannot fix the volume depletion — both are needed.

opts.text: D50W 25 g IV push for glucose 52, then recheck q15min and repeat as needed
opts.rat: Glucose <70 in a symptomatic client requires immediate correction. EMS already gave one dose; recheck and repeat if recurrent. Long-term coverage comes from the continuous D5-containing fluids.

opts.text: Calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly for hyperkalemia with ECG changes
opts.rat: K+ 6.4 with peaked T-waves and prolonged PR mandates membrane stabilization. Onset 1–3 min; duration ~30–60 min. Does NOT lower K+ but protects cardiac membrane while other interventions take effect. Use calcium GLUCONATE preferentially (less tissue irritation than calcium chloride for peripheral IV).

opts.text: Regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25 g IV simultaneously to shift K+ intracellularly
opts.rat: Drives K+ into cells within minutes. The D50W simultaneously addresses the hypoglycemia AND prevents iatrogenic hypoglycemia from insulin. Albuterol nebulized adds additional beta-2-mediated shift.

opts.text: Blood cultures x2 from separate peripheral sites before any antibiotics
opts.rat: Sepsis is a known precipitant of adrenal crisis in chronic AI clients (and can occur concurrently). The viral gastroenteritis history is most likely the trigger, but bacterial superinfection or unrelated sepsis must be excluded. If any sepsis signs emerge, treat empirically with broad-spectrum antibiotics.

opts.text: Withhold hydrocortisone until the cortisol level returns from the lab to confirm the diagnosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — most important trap in this case. Adrenal crisis kills via delay. Treat based on clinical suspicion. Confirmatory testing follows. Many providers reflexively wait for labs in non-emergent situations; this is one where you cannot.

opts.text: Norepinephrine drip at 0.1 mcg/kg/min as the FIRST-line agent for refractory hypotension
opts.rat: Vasopressors are not first-line in adrenal crisis because vascular beds are hyporesponsive without cortisol. Give hydrocortisone first. If hypotension persists 30–60 min after steroids and adequate volume, norepinephrine becomes appropriate as second-line (and will work much better with cortisol on board).

opts.text: Rapid correction of sodium from 122 to 138 mEq/L over 4 hours with 3% hypertonic saline
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUS — central pontine myelinolysis risk. Sodium correction should not exceed 8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours (some guidelines stricter, 6 mEq/L/24hr in chronic hyponatremia). 3% saline is reserved for severely symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, coma, herniation). This client's mental status will improve with treatment of the underlying crisis.

opts.text: IV potassium replacement to 'protect against arrhythmias'
opts.rat: ⚠️ CATASTROPHIC — K+ is critically HIGH at 6.4. Adding more potassium would cause immediate arrhythmia. The mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary AI causes K+ retention, the OPPOSITE of what occurs in chronic loop diuretic users. Always check the actual K+ before assuming.

opts.text: Discharge home once mental status improves with instructions to resume oral hydrocortisone tomorrow
opts.rat: Premature and unsafe. Client needs: (1) ICU/IMC observation through resolution of the crisis, (2) IV steroids for 24–48 hours, (3) gradual taper to oral at 2–3x maintenance dose, (4) complete sick day rule education, (5) injectable hydrocortisone emergency kit, (6) plan for outpatient endocrine follow-up, (7) reinforcement of medical alert bracelet.

opts.text: Discharge teaching: sick day rules (double or triple maintenance dose during minor illness, switch to IV/IM for severe vomiting), Solu-Cortef 100 mg IM emergency kit, medical alert bracelet, low threshold to return to ED
opts.rat: Critical for safe disposition and recurrence prevention. The reason this crisis occurred was lack of stress dosing during illness. Every chronic steroid user should leave the hospital with: written sick day rules, an injectable hydrocortisone kit they (or family) can administer at home, and a medical alert bracelet or wallet card.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: What is the FIRST action sequence?
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the nurse should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Call rapid response / endocrine / ICU; ensure team mobilization; confirm IV access patent; do not leave the client
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Adrenal crisis is a time-sensitive emergency requiring multidisciplinary care. Confirming IV access ensures the next step (hydrocortisone) can be given without delay. Do not leave the client alone — mental status can deteriorate rapidly.

opts.text: Draw cortisol and ACTH levels (single venipuncture if possible), then administer hydrocortisone 100 mg IV PUSH immediately
opts.rat: #2 — Time-critical. Cortisol/ACTH BEFORE steroid if access allows — provides diagnostic confirmation. Then IV push hydrocortisone immediately. If drawing labs would take more than 2–3 minutes, give the hydrocortisone FIRST and draw labs after. Saving the life trumps confirming the diagnosis.

opts.text: Administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly for hyperkalemia with ECG changes; follow with regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25 g IV
opts.rat: #3 — Concurrent emergency. Hyperkalemic ECG changes mandate immediate membrane stabilization and K+ shifting. Acts within minutes. Buys time while hydrocortisone and definitive K+ removal (kayexalate, dialysis if needed) take effect.

opts.text: Initiate 0.9% NS 1 L IV bolus, then D5NS at 200 mL/hr; check POC glucose q15min and give D50W IV push if glucose <70
opts.rat: #4 — Volume + glucose support. Restores intravascular volume (working with the hydrocortisone-restored vascular tone). D5 component prevents recurrent hypoglycemia. Frequent glucose monitoring catches recurrence before symptoms.

opts.text: Insert Foley for hourly UOP monitoring; draw remaining labs (CBC, ABG, lactate, blood cultures, TSH); arrange ICU transfer; begin stress-dose education with family
opts.rat: #5 — Sustained management. Foley quantifies fluid response. Labs identify precipitating sepsis and concurrent conditions. ICU transfer ensures ongoing high-acuity care. Beginning education early (especially with the daughter present) starts the safe-discharge preparation.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1 hour post-hydrocortisone: BP 104/68, HR 96, mental status clearing to A&Ox4, ECG with normalized T-waves, glucose 98
opts.rat: TEXTBOOK successful resuscitation. Hydrocortisone restored vascular tone (BP up, HR down as compensation resolves); cardiac stabilization resolved the K+ ECG changes; volume + D5 corrected glucose. The dramatic clinical response within 1 hour is characteristic of adrenal crisis and is sometimes diagnostic in itself.

opts.text: 4 hours post: Na trending up to 130, K+ down to 4.8 after combined treatment, lactate cleared to 1.4, UOP 70 mL/hr; transferred to IMC
opts.rat: Excellent trajectory across all parameters. Sodium correction is appropriate (122 → 130 over 4 hours = 8 mEq/L/4hr — actually slightly fast; slow down maintenance fluids to ensure correction <8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours total). K+ down through both hydrocortisone (mineralocorticoid effect) and insulin shift.

opts.text: Day 3: transitioned to PO hydrocortisone 40 mg AM + 20 mg PM (2x maintenance) with planned taper to baseline over 5–7 days; receives Solu-Cortef 100 mg IM kit, family demonstrates kit use, reinforced medical alert bracelet, scheduled endo follow-up in 1 week
opts.rat: Comprehensive disposition. The IM emergency kit is the single most impactful intervention to prevent the next crisis — the family can now administer it if she's vomiting at home. Plan for endocrine follow-up ensures ongoing optimization. Cortisol and ACTH (drawn pre-hydrocortisone) returned at 1.2 mcg/dL and 580 pg/mL respectively — confirming primary AI.

opts.text: Despite hydrocortisone, 3 L NS, and norepinephrine 0.4 mcg/kg/min, BP remains 78/46 with rising lactate 5.4 and oliguria
opts.rat: Refractory shock despite combination therapy. Consider: (1) concurrent septic shock — broaden antibiotics, add vasopressin; (2) inadequate steroid dose — increase to 200 mg/24h continuous infusion or 50 mg IV q4h; (3) other shock causes (cardiogenic from MI in older client). Aggressive workup with bedside echo and consideration of mechanical circulatory support.

opts.text: Develops new focal seizure activity with right-sided weakness; head CT shows osmotic demyelination changes in the pons
opts.rat: Central Pontine Myelinolysis (osmotic demyelination syndrome) from too-rapid sodium correction. Devastating, often irreversible. PREVENTABLE: sodium correction must not exceed 8–10 mEq/L per 24 hours. If overcorrection occurs, can cautiously give free water (D5W) to lower sodium back down within first 24 hours. This is the worst preventable complication of adrenal crisis management.

opts.text: Blood cultures grow E. coli at 24 hours; client has been on empiric ceftriaxone since admission; UA confirms pyelonephritis; antibiotics narrowed
opts.rat: Concurrent urosepsis was contributing to crisis. Empiric broad-spectrum coverage from admission caught it; identification allows narrowing (de-escalation = antimicrobial stewardship). Clinical response confirms both adrenal crisis AND sepsis were treated. Common pattern — sepsis precipitates crisis in chronic AI clients.

opts.text: Day 1: develops new chest pain, ECG with ST elevations II, III, aVF; troponin rises; emergent cath shows RCA occlusion
opts.rat: Acute inferior MI — can both precipitate adrenal crisis (massive stress) and occur secondary to the crisis (demand ischemia from hypotension + tachycardia in known CAD patient). Activate cath lab. Continue hydrocortisone — steroid pretreatment is actually protective in cardiac surgery and possibly in ACS as well.

opts.text: Mild residual fatigue and weakness expected for several days; advised gradual return to baseline activity
opts.rat: Expected post-crisis course. The body needs time to recalibrate after a major stress event. Counsel client and family that fatigue may last 1–2 weeks; gradual return to activity is appropriate. Importantly, this fatigue is NOT a sign of recurrent crisis — true crisis would have hemodynamic and electrolyte derangements.

opts.text: Hyperpigmentation of buccal mucosa, palmar creases, and old scar unchanged on discharge
opts.rat: Expected. Hyperpigmentation in primary AI is a chronic finding from years of elevated ACTH/MSH. It does NOT reverse with acute hydrocortisone treatment. Long-term, with optimal replacement and proper ACTH suppression, pigmentation may slowly fade — but this takes months to years.

opts.text: Continued need for IV hydrocortisone for 24–48 hours before transitioning to oral; family completes return-demonstration of injectable hydrocortisone IM kit administration
opts.rat: Expected discharge preparation course. Education and demonstration ensure that if she ever vomits/decompensates at home again, the family can give IM Solu-Cortef and call 911. This single intervention dramatically reduces recurrent crisis mortality.
